stories.title: Singapore's Big Banks Hit Their Highest Prices Ever
stories.headline_kid: DBS, OCBC and UOB — Singapore's three biggest banks — have never been worth more on the stock market!
summary: Singapore's three biggest banks — DBS, OCBC and UOB — have share prices at record highs, meaning they have never been valued this much before. A 'share' is like owning a tiny slice of a company. When the company does well, your tiny slice becomes worth more money.

These banks are earning more profit these days, partly because of how interest rates work and because more people are saving and borrowing money through them. Markets across Asia also got a boost this week as technology company shares bounced back and oil prices eased slightly.

But experts say deciding whether to buy, hold, or sell these bank shares isn't just about the price going up. It depends on why someone bought the shares in the first place — are they saving for many years, or hoping for quick profit?
This matters for families because many parents' savings, insurance plans, or CPF-linked funds are connected to these banks. When the banks do well, it can be good news for household finances too.

lenses.me: You don't need to buy shares to understand this — but knowing what a 'share' is can help you make sense of grown-up money talk.
lenses.family: Your parents' savings, insurance, or CPF might be linked to these banks, so good bank performance can be good news for your family.
lenses.friends: You might hear classmates mention their parents talking about 'the stock market' — now you'll know what it means!
lenses.nation: Strong banks are important for Singapore's reputation as a trusted global financial hub.
lenses.world: Singapore's bank shares rise and fall along with what happens in bigger markets like the US and other parts of Asia, showing how connected the world's economy is.

compo_words.meaning: the highest or best ever achieved
compo_words.example: The runner set a record time in the school race.

compo_words.meaning: a person who studies information carefully to give advice
compo_words.example: The analyst explained why the price of rice had gone up.

compo_words.meaning: to put money into something hoping it grows over time
compo_words.example: My uncle likes to invest his savings instead of spending it all.

compo_words.meaning: the extra money earned after paying all costs
compo_words.example: The bakery made a good profit from selling bread this month.

compo_words.meaning: a feeling of trust that something will succeed
compo_words.example: Investors showed confidence in the company by buying more shares.

compo_phrases.meaning: the highest it has ever been
compo_phrases.example: Ticket prices for the concert were at an all-time high.
My mother told me to keep an eye on the stock prices before deciding.

questions: Have you ever heard your parents talk about money, savings, or the stock market at home?
What do you think it means to 'invest' your money instead of just saving it?
If you had some savings, would you keep it in a piggy bank or try to grow it? Why?
phrases: I think this is important because...
If I were in this situation, I would...

sources.name: CNA - Singapore bank stocks record highs

stories.title: Court Punishes Gang Who Sold Babies to Singapore
stories.headline_kid: An Indonesian court sent 19 people to jail for a terrible crime — selling babies, with some ending up in Singapore.
summary: A court in Indonesia has sentenced 19 people to between 3 and 7 years in jail. They were found guilty of selling at least 34 babies between 2022 and 2025. Some of these babies were taken all the way to Singapore and sold for thousands of dollars each.

Selling a person — even a tiny baby — is called human trafficking, and it is a very serious crime everywhere in the world. It treats a human being like an object to be bought and sold, which is deeply wrong. Many of the babies likely came from mothers who were struggling and were taken advantage of by these criminals.

This case shows how important it is for countries like Singapore and Indonesia to work together to catch criminals who operate across borders. Investigators are still working to make sure every baby involved is safe and properly cared for.
The judges gave jail sentences to show how seriously the law treats this crime. More investigations may follow to find out if any other babies from this network still need to be found.

lenses.me: It's a reminder that every baby and child deserves love, safety, and protection — never to be treated unfairly.
lenses.family: Your family might talk about how adoption should always be done properly and legally, never through illegal deals.
lenses.friends: This story might spark a discussion with friends about why laws protect children so strongly.
lenses.nation: It shows Singapore and Indonesia's police working together to fight serious cross-border crimes.
lenses.world: Human trafficking is a global problem, and this case shows why countries must cooperate to stop it.

compo_words.meaning: illegally moving or selling people, often for money
compo_words.example: Human trafficking is a serious crime punished by law.

compo_words.meaning: officially found guilty of a crime in court
compo_words.example: The gang members were convicted after a long trial.

compo_words.meaning: easily hurt or taken advantage of
compo_words.example: The charity helps vulnerable families in need.

compo_words.meaning: the punishment given by a court
compo_words.example: The judge gave a jail sentence of five years.

compo_words.meaning: to unfairly use someone for personal gain
compo_words.example: It is wrong to exploit people who are struggling.

compo_phrases.meaning: punished by law for doing wrong
compo_phrases.example: The criminals were finally brought to justice after the trial.

compo_phrases.meaning: an illegal act that happens across more than one country
compo_phrases.example: Cross-border crime needs cooperation between different police forces.

questions: Why do you think laws protect babies and children so strongly?
How do you think countries can work together to stop crimes like this?
What would you say to a friend who asks why some babies are adopted from other countries?

sources.name: CNA - Indonesia baby trafficking ring jailed

stories.title: Unusual 'Future School' in Johor Ordered to Close
stories.headline_kid: A strange, futuristic school built in Forest City, just across the water from Singapore, has been ordered to shut down.
summary: Forest City is a huge, futuristic city built on reclaimed land in Johor, Malaysia — very close to Singapore, just across the Second Link. Many tall towers were built there, though the city is still fairly empty.

Inside Forest City, a group of tech entrepreneurs set up something called 'Network School' — a new kind of school without normal classrooms, aiming to teach students in a different way. It attracted attention from around the world.

But local authorities in Iskandar Puteri revoked the school's business licence, which means it lost its official permission to operate. Johor's Chief Minister said the decision followed existing laws, since even unusual businesses like this need proper approval to run.
This matters to Singapore because Forest City sits right on our doorstep. Many Singaporeans have visited it, own property there, or are curious about how this ambitious project is developing.

lenses.me: It shows that even exciting new ideas — like a 'school of the future' — must still follow proper rules to operate.
lenses.family: If your family has ever visited or considered property in Forest City, this news is directly relevant.
lenses.friends: It's a fun topic to discuss — what would your dream school of the future look like?
lenses.nation: Singapore and Malaysia are close neighbours, so developments just across the border in Johor often affect us too.
lenses.world: It's a reminder that even bold, futuristic projects everywhere must follow their country's laws.

compo_words.example: The young entrepreneur started an online bakery.

compo_phrases.example: The factory had to cease operations after the fire.

compo_phrases.example: The decision was made in accordance with the law.

What do you think an ideal 'school of the future' should look like?

stories.title: Scam Gangs Steal a Massive US$88 Billion Across Asia
stories.headline_kid: Criminal gangs across Asia stole a huge amount of money through online scams last year — Singapore families should stay alert!
summary: A United Nations report says crime gangs across the Asia-Pacific region stole about US$88 billion (nearly S$114 billion) through online scams. That's an almost impossible-to-imagine amount of money — enough to build thousands of schools.

These scams often use fake job offers, romance tricks, fake investments, or phishing messages that try to steal your family's passwords or bank details. Sadly, some of these scams are run from secret centres where workers are forced to trick people.
The UN warns these criminal networks are growing bigger and working together across different countries and different types of crimes, making it much harder for police everywhere to stop them.

Singapore is often targeted by scam calls and messages too. Police remind everyone — including kids — to never share OTP codes or personal information, and to always check with a trusted adult if something feels suspicious.

lenses.me: Never click on suspicious links or share passwords, even if a message looks friendly or urgent.
lenses.family: Talk with your family about scam warning signs, especially for grandparents who may be more easily tricked.
lenses.friends: Tell a friend if you see a suspicious message or a 'too good to be true' offer online.
lenses.nation: Singapore has an Anti-Scam Centre and works with other countries to catch these criminal networks.
lenses.world: This shows how scams have become a huge, borderless crime problem that needs countries to work together to fight.

compo_words.meaning: an organised group of criminals working together
compo_words.example: Police busted a scam syndicate operating online.

compo_words.meaning: tricking people online to steal their information
compo_words.example: She almost fell for a phishing email pretending to be her bank.

compo_words.meaning: always watchful and alert for danger
compo_words.example: We must stay vigilant against strangers online.

compo_words.meaning: to unfairly take advantage of someone
compo_words.example: Scammers exploit people's trust to steal money.

compo_words.meaning: a group of connected people or things working together
compo_words.example: The police broke up a large scam network.

compo_phrases.meaning: something that seems too great to actually be real, often a warning sign
compo_phrases.example: Winning a prize you never entered sounds too good to be true.
We should stay vigilant when using the internet.

questions: Has your family ever received a suspicious call, text or email?
What would you do if a stranger online asked for your parents' bank details?
Why do you think it's important to double-check before clicking on a link?
phrases: My family always tells me to...
I think the best way to stay safe is...
If this happened to me, I would...

sources.name: CNA - Crime gangs scams Asia-Pacific UN

stories.title: Delivery Rider by Day, Award-Winning Poet by Night
stories.headline_kid: A food delivery rider in China proved that big dreams can come true — he just won China's top poetry prize!
summary: Wang Jibing, a 56-year-old food delivery rider from Jiangsu province in China, has won the Lu Xun Literary Prize — one of China's most respected writing awards. He won it for his poetry collection called 'Low Flight'.

By day, Wang zooms around delivering food on his scooter. By night, he writes poems about the tiring but meaningful life of delivery riders — their hopes, their tired legs, and small moments of beauty during a busy shift.

One line from his poem imagines a special 'fifth season' just for delivery riders — their own kind of spring. His story reminds us that people doing ordinary jobs can still have extraordinary talents and dreams.

Even in Singapore, we see delivery riders every day bringing us bubble tea or dinner. Wang's story is a gentle reminder to appreciate their hard work — and to remember that everyone, no matter their job, might be chasing a passion of their own.

lenses.me: If you have a hobby or dream you love, this story shows that trying hard and never giving up can lead somewhere amazing.
lenses.family: It's a good chance to talk about being kind and patient with delivery riders who bring food to your home.
lenses.friends: You could ask your classmates if they have any hidden talents or hobbies they're passionate about too.
lenses.nation: Singapore also has many delivery riders working hard every day — this story helps us see and appreciate them more.
lenses.world: It shows that creativity and talent can be found in any job, in any country, no matter how ordinary it seems.

compo_words.meaning: greatly respected and admired
compo_words.example: She won a prestigious award for her science project.

compo_words.meaning: a group of things, like poems, gathered together
compo_words.example: He published a collection of short stories.

compo_words.meaning: continuing to try despite difficulty
compo_words.example: Her persistence in practising paid off during the competition.

compo_words.meaning: to encourage someone to do or feel something positive
compo_words.example: His story inspired me to keep working on my drawings.

compo_words.meaning: normal, not unusual or special
compo_words.example: Even an ordinary day can have special moments.

compo_phrases.example: Against all odds, he managed to finish his poetry collection.

compo_phrases.meaning: to do many different jobs or roles
compo_phrases.example: As a rider and poet, he wears many hats.

questions: Do you have a hobby or dream that you keep working on, even when you're busy or tired?

phrases: One thing I learnt from this story is...
